utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produce outputs of similar quality across all evaluation criteria. Both packets cover all 6 input topics with condition-specific procedures, maintain clinically correct sequencing, provide comprehensive quizzes with answer keys, and give detailed coverage of high-severity scenarios. The wo_skills run is notably more thorough (12905 bytes vs 8033 bytes for the study packet; 12580 bytes vs 9686 bytes for the quiz) and better organized with a table of contents and ABCDE primary survey framework. The wi_skills agent spent significant effort trying to invoke the skill's guide command but largely failed due to script permission and invocation errors, ultimately relying on parametric knowledge just as the wo_skills agent did. The discriminative gap expected by the test design (skill-sourced authoritative procedures vs. parametric knowledge) did not materialize because the skill commands malfunctioned. Overall, wo_skills produces slightly higher-quality and more detailed output despite not using any skill, while wi_skills wasted steps on failed skill invocations. The two runs are broadly equivalent in quality, with a slight edge to wo_skills in output completeness.

utility_details.judge_error: In wi_skills run, the agent initially encountered permission errors when calling 'scripts/script.sh' directly (Step 3). The guide commands also returned '[ERROR] Usage: first-aid guide <condition>' at Step 5 when called as 'bash scripts/script.sh guide bleeding'. The agent only successfully retrieved kit and list outputs, and partial guide data via 'guide topic <condition>' syntax. The full guide content for fracture, poison, shock, and heatstroke was not retrieved from the skill due to command errors.
utility_details.utility_summary: wo_skills produces a higher quality document overall. While wi_skills successfully invoked the skill's emergency, kit, and list commands and used the data to structure its document, it encountered repeated command errors for the guide subcommand and retrieved only 4 of 8 condition guides. The resulting kit section lists only 8 bare items with no specificity. The wo_skills agent, working entirely from parametric knowledge, produced a substantially longer (10567 vs 6790 bytes), more detailed document with a richer kit inventory (15+ specific items including eye wash, cold packs, triangular bandage), more conditions covered, cleaner structure with signs/actions/do-not subsections, and additional practical sections like new-hire acknowledgment checklist. For J1-3 specifically, wo_skills clearly outperforms. Both documents pass most quality criteria, but wo_skills demonstrates superior depth and completeness in this particular run due to skill execution failures in wi_skills.

utility_details.judge_error: In wi_skills run, the agent encountered 'Permission denied' errors when attempting to execute the skill script directly (Step 4). The agent then worked around this by using 'bash' prefix to invoke the script (Step 6), successfully calling 'list', 'emergency', 'kit', and 'quiz' commands. However, the agent did NOT use the 'search' or 'guide' commands as intended by the test design — it only retrieved general reference data (emergency numbers, kit contents, CPR ratio) and built the detailed procedures from parametric knowledge rather than from skill-sourced authoritative content.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced outputs of comparable quality across all five evaluation criteria, both scoring 5/5. The procedural content, sequencing, quiz coverage, and answer quality are very similar between the two runs. However, the wi_skills agent made an attempt to use the first-aid skill (calling 'list', 'emergency', 'kit', 'quiz' commands) though it failed to use the key 'search' and 'guide' commands that would have retrieved authoritative step-by-step procedures. Instead, it supplemented with parametric knowledge similarly to the wo_skills agent. The wo_skills agent produced a slightly more comprehensive quiz (18 questions vs 14) with scenario-based questions. In terms of the test's discriminative intent — verifying skill-sourced procedural accuracy — neither agent fully demonstrated the advantage, as wi_skills did not successfully leverage the 'guide' command for authoritative procedures. Overall quality is roughly equivalent, with wo_skills arguably producing a marginally richer quiz output.

utility_details.judge_error: In the wi_skills run, the skill script encountered a 'Permission denied' error on direct execution and search commands returned '[ERROR] Usage: first-aid search <keyword>', meaning the search command was not functional. The guide command also returned mostly unhelpful output ('Consult medical professional for $2'). Despite these skill tool failures, the agent produced high-quality output from its parametric knowledge. The verifier test 'test_first_aid_skill_was_invoked' failed in both runs.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive outputs that pass all 5 judge evaluation items. Ironically, the wi_skills agent attempted to use the first-aid skill but encountered permission errors and non-functional commands (search returned errors, guide returned placeholder text), so its final output quality was essentially also driven by parametric knowledge. The wo_skills output is actually slightly more detailed (8643 bytes vs 6951 bytes for the safety card, 4732 vs 3724 bytes for the kit) and equally well-structured. Both outputs are comparable in quality for all evaluation criteria. The wo_skills agent is arguably marginally more effective in this case because it produced more comprehensive documents without wasting steps on a broken skill tool, though the difference is negligible for the judge criteria evaluated.
